Mercedes‑Benz B180 / B200 Petrol Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz B180 / B200 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B180/B200 petrol engine typically costs £2,800–£4,500 supply only, with the M270 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. A used M266 engine is available from approximately £600–£1,200. A new OEM replacement engine can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your B‑Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz B180 / B200 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine era your B‑Class has. The W245 (2005–2011) with the M266 is a naturally aspirated engine that can suffer from timing chain stretch and valve coking. The W246 (2011–2018) with the M270 is a 1.6‑litre turbocharged direct‑injection engine that is more powerful and efficient, but has well‑documented issues: timing chain wear at around 80,000 miles, camshaft actuator and tone ring failure, thermostat failure, and carbon buildup on intake valves. With regular maintenance and proactive timing chain attention, the M270 can be a reliable engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz B180 / B200 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are timing chain wear on the M270 (rattling on cold start — can destroy the engine if ignored), camshaft actuator and tone ring failure on the M270 (rough idle, warning lights), thermostat failure on the M270 (slow warm‑up, poor fuel economy), carbon buildup on intake valves on the M270 (direct‑injection trait), and timing chain stretch on the M266 (W245). A recall was also issued for some 2019‑2020 M282‑engined models regarding a turbocharger oil feed line leak.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a B180 / B200 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and engine era. Against a well‑kept W246 worth £6,000–£12,000, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 30–50% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired W245 worth £2,000–£4,000, the same recon may exceed our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. M270‑engined cars are more desirable due to their turbocharged performance and modern features.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz B180 / B200 petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: W245 (2005–2011): M266 (1.7L for B180, 2.0L for B200). W246 (2011–2018): M270 (1.6L turbo). Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M270 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and tensioner must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £1,000–£2,500. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £3,500–£6,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: B‑Class B180 / B200 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch it before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Catch it early — before timing slips
Catch it early — far cheaper than replacement

buyingChecks.title: Buying This B180 / B200 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * W246 (2011–2018) with documented timing chain inspection or replacement
* W245 with full service history and regular oil changes
* No cold‑start rattling (timing chain health — M270)
* No rough idle or warning lights (camshaft actuator health — M270)
* Evidence of regular longer runs (prevents carbon buildup on direct‑injection engines)
avoidIf: * W246 with cold‑start rattle and no chain history — assume it's overdue
* W245 with no service history — the M266 is sensitive to oil quality
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, camshaft or sensor problems
* Short‑journey ownership history — carbon buildup likely on direct‑injection M270

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The B‑Class B180 and B200 petrol are Mercedes‑Benz's compact MPV petrol offerings across two generations. The W245 (2005–2011) uses the M266 — a naturally aspirated engine that suffers from timing chain stretch and valve coking. The W246 (2011–2018) uses the M270 — a 1.6‑litre turbocharged direct‑injection engine that offers significantly more power (120–156 bhp) and efficiency. The M270 has well‑documented issues: timing chain wear at around 80,000 miles, camshaft actuator and tone ring failure, and thermostat failure. With typical B‑Class values ranging from £3,000 (W245) to £12,000+ (W246), an engine replacement is a significant investment — a well‑maintained W246 M270 with documented timing chain history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare B180 / B200 petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz B‑Class B180 / B200 Petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain wear warning — M270 particularly) — see the map above
* Service History — Including which engine era (M266 or M270) and oil‑change discipline
* Timing Chain — For M270 (W246): check for any chain work or cold‑start rattle
* Camshaft Actuator — Check for smooth idle and acceleration; any roughness may indicate actuator issues
* Thermostat — Check warm‑up time; slow warm‑up may indicate thermostat failure
* Carbon Buildup — On M270 (direct‑injection), listen for rough idle; may require walnut blasting
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, camshaft or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
